docs.title: VCF Networking Configuration Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the VCF Networking configuration guide. This guide provides step-by-step instructions for configuring network services across different VCF Components.

docs.title: Choose your VCF component
docs.text:
Select the VCF component you are using to configure your network services.

  • vCenter Server Best for VI Admins. Go to vCenter Server section

  • VCF Automation (VCF-A) Best for Cloud Admins. Go to VCF Automation section

Document Versioning

This guide is updated for VCF 9.1+. If you are running an older version, some options may not be available.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
vCenter Network Services

This section describes the procedures for configuring network services via the vSphere Client.

docs.title: VPC Network Services
docs.text:
Explore the specific configuration guides for each VPC network service:

  • VPC Router Logical router for VPC networking.
  • VPC Subnet Logical Subnet (to connect VMs/K8s workloads). Optionally, a Subnet-VLAN can also be created.
  • NAT Supports multiple NAT configurations: . External-IP (1:1 NAT) . Outbound-NAT (N:1 NAT) . NAT (SNAT/DNAT)
  • DHCP DHCP services can be configured as: . DHCP Server (managed by VCF) . DHCP Relay (using an external DHCP server such as Infoblox)
  • Load Balancer VMware AVI Load Balancer. Configuration not available from vCenter (available through AVI).
  • VPN Secure Site-to-Site connectivity. Configuration not available from vCenter (available through NSX).

Prerequisite: vCenter must be VPC-ready

Before configuring VPC network services, the VPC Network Connectivity (Centralized or Distributed) must be configured. See section North VPC Connectivity Configuration below to prepare the vCenter environment.

docs.title: North VPC Connectivity Configuration
docs.text:
Explore the configuration guides for each North VPC connectivity component:

Document Versioning

This guide is updated for VCF 9.1+. If you are running an older version, some options may not be available.

docs.title: VCF Network Infrastructure
docs.text:
• Edge Cluster / Edge Node NSX Edge appliances providing centralized network services for Central Transit Gateway Designs.
  • VNA Cluster / VNA Node NSX Virtual Network appliances providing centralized network services for Distributed Transit Gateway Designs.
  • Tier-0 / BGP Tier-0 logical router providing connectivity between Centralized Transit Gateways and the physical network.

docs.title: VCF North Connectivity
docs.text:
• External Connection Connection between the NSX environment and the physical network.
  • Transit Gateway Logical router connecting VPC networks to external physical networks.
  • IP Blocks External (Infoblox) + TGW Priv. IP blocks used for VPC subnet allocation. Not represented in the diagram.
  • Network Span Defines how VPC subnets span across vCenter clusters. Not represented in the diagram.
  • Connectivity Policy Defines cross-VPC communication rules. Not represented in the diagram.

? Connectivity Pofile ?

docs.title: VPC Router
docs.text:
VPC Router

This section describes the procedures for configuring a VPC Router using the vSphere Client.

docs.title: 2. Choose the VPC Router name (+ IP Block + North connectivity + Cross-VPC communication)
docs.text:
• Private - VPC IP CIDRs: (Optional) IP Block reserved for future VPC Private-VPC subnets. The IP Block is flexible because VMs connected to Private VPC subnets are always NATed with a public IP when accessing external networks.

  • Connectivity Profile Select the Connectivity Profile (the profile containing the Transit Gateway + Span). This defines how the VPC connects to the physical network.

  • Connectivity Policy Select the Connectivity Policy (rules governing cross-VPC communication). This determines whether communication with other VPC subnets is allowed or denied.

docs.title: Result - Topology
docs.text: You can see the VPC-Router in a graphical way under Topology:

docs.title: VPC Subnet
docs.text:
VPC Subnet

This section describes the procedures for configuring a VPC Subnet using the vSphere Client.

docs.title: 2. Choose the VPC Subnet name (+ VLAN option + Subnet mode + IP Block & Size + Connectivity + DHCP)
docs.text:
• VLAN Extensions: Disabled for the VPC-Subnet Overlay.
  • Access Mode Select VPC Subnet mode based (Public / Private TGW / Private VPC) on the VM access you need.

Click on image below to Zoom in.

  • Connectivity Policy Select the Connectivity Policy (Policy for cross-VPC communication). That's to allow/deny communication to other VPC subnets.

docs.title: NAT Configuration in vCenter
docs.text: Use this section to configure Network Address Translation (NAT) for your VPC workloads. NAT allows private subnets to communicate with external networks or provides a way for external users to reach internal services.

docs.title: Overview of NAT Types
docs.text: Type Use Case SNAT Source NAT: Allows VMs on private subnets to reach the Internet. DNAT Destination NAT: Allows external users to reach a specific VM (e.g., a Web Server). Reflexive Stateless NAT: One-to-one mapping between a private and public IP.

docs.title: Configuration Steps
docs.text:
Choose your preferred method for configuring NAT rules.

vCenter UIPowerVCF / CLI
  1. Log in to vCenter Server.
  2. Navigate to Inventory > Networking.
  3. Select your VPC from the list.
  4. Click the Configure tab and select Network Services > NAT.
  5. Click Add Rule and fill in the details:
    • Name: Production-SNAT
    • Type: SNAT
    • External IP: Select an IP from your public block.

To automate NAT creation, use the following API call structure:

POST /api/v1/vpc/nat-rules
{
    "display_name": "Production-SNAT",
    "action": "SNAT",
    "translated_network": "192.168.1.0/24",
    "public_ip": "10.0.0.50"
}

Routing Dependency

Before NAT will function, ensure your **Tier-0

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
VCF Automation Network Services

This section provides technical procedures for configuring and managing network services via the VCF Automation.

docs.title: VPC Network Services - VCF-A Tenant
docs.text:
Explore the specific configuration guides for each VPC network service available to the VCF-A Tenants:

  • VPC Router Logical router.
  • VPC Subnet Logical Subnet (for VMs/K8s connection). Option to also create Subnet-VLAN.
  • NAT External-IP (1:1 NAT) or Outbound-NAT (N:1 NAT) or NAT (SNAT/DNAT)
  • DHCP DHCP Server (managed by VCF) or DHCP Relay (managed by 3rd party DHCP Server like Infoblox)
  • Load Balancer xxx.
  • VPN Secure Site-to-Site connectivity.

docs.title: North VPC Connectivity Configuration - VCF-A Provider
docs.text:
Explore the specific configuration guides for each North VPC Connectivity Configuration:

Document Versioning

This guide is updated for VCF 9.1+. If you are running an older version, some options may not be available.

docs.title: VCF Network Infrastructure
docs.text:
• Edge Cluster / Edge Node NSX Edge appliances providing centralized network services for Central Transit Gateway Designs.
  • VNA Cluster / VNA Node NSX Virtual Network appliances providing centralized network services for Distributed Transit Gateway Designs.
  • Tier-0 / BGP Tier-0 logical router providing connectivity between Centralized Transit Gateways and the physical network.
  • Subnet-VLAN VLAN-backed VPC subnets.

docs.title: VCF North Connectivity
docs.text:
• External Connection Connection between the NSX environment and the physical network.
  • Transit Gateway Logical router connecting VPC networks to external physical networks.
  • IP Blocks External (Infoblox) + TGW Priv. IP blocks used for VPC subnet allocation. Not represented in the diagram.
  • Network Span Defines how VPC subnets span across vCenter clusters. Not represented in the diagram. Configuration not available from VCF Automation (available from vCenter or NSX).
  • Community Policy Defines cross-VPC communication options. Not represented in the diagram. Configuration not available from VCF Automation (available from vCenter or NSX).

docs.title: VPC Subnet
docs.text:
vCenter UIAPI / PowerVCF
  1. Go to Networking.
  2. Click NAT.
POST /api/v1/nat-rules

docs.title: NAT Configuration in vCenter
docs.text: Use this section to configure Network Address Translation (NAT) for your VPC workloads. NAT allows private subnets to communicate with external networks or provides a way for external users to reach internal services.

docs.title: Overview of NAT Types
docs.text: Type Use Case SNAT Source NAT: Allows VMs on private subnets to reach the Internet. DNAT Destination NAT: Allows external users to reach a specific VM (e.g., a Web Server). Reflexive Stateless NAT: One-to-one mapping between a private and public IP.

docs.title: Configuration Steps
docs.text:
Choose your preferred method for configuring NAT rules.

vCenter UIPowerVCF / CLI
  1. Log in to vCenter Server.
  2. Navigate to Inventory > Networking.
  3. Select your VPC from the list.
  4. Click the Configure tab and select Network Services > NAT.
  5. Click Add Rule and fill in the details:
    • Name: Production-SNAT
    • Type: SNAT
    • External IP: Select an IP from your public block.

To automate NAT creation, use the following API call structure:

POST /api/v1/vpc/nat-rules
{
    "display_name": "Production-SNAT",
    "action": "SNAT",
    "translated_network": "192.168.1.0/24",
    "public_ip": "10.0.0.50"
}
